findings.title: No authentication or authorization implementation anywhere in the task plan
findings.description: The entire task plan covers a marketing website and infrastructure scaling but includes zero tasks for authentication, authorization, user session management, or RBAC. The /api/snapshot endpoint is the only API route and has no auth protection. While the marketing site itself may not need user auth, the scale tasks deploy production infrastructure (NATS, ingress, Kubernetes) without any mention of application-level auth for services that will presumably run on this infrastructure. If any future API routes or admin interfaces are added, there is no foundation for auth.
findings.recommendation: Add a task for establishing an authentication framework (e.g., NextAuth.js, Clerk, or custom JWT middleware) even if initially only applied to diagnostic endpoints. For NATS (task 1012/1013), ensure client authentication is configured (NATS supports token, user/password, and NKey auth) — the current plan mentions TLS but not client authentication. Add NATS authorization configuration to restrict which services can publish/subscribe to which subjects.

findings.title: NATS credentials and secrets management not addressed
findings.description: Task 1013 mentions documenting 'NATS endpoint URLs and credentials for application teams' but there is no task for secrets management infrastructure. No mention of Kubernetes Secrets, external secrets operators (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), secret rotation, or how TLS certificates/private keys will be stored and rotated. Task 1013 mentions cert-manager for TLS but doesn't address the broader secrets lifecycle.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated task for secrets management: (1) Deploy an external secrets operator or configure Kubernetes Secrets with encryption at rest. (2) Store NATS credentials, TLS private keys, and any API keys in a secrets management solution. (3) Configure automatic secret rotation for NATS credentials and TLS certificates. (4) Never document credentials in plain text — instead document how to retrieve them from the secrets manager.

findings.title: /api/snapshot endpoint leaks internal architecture information
findings.description: The /api/snapshot endpoint returns the component tree structure and token validation status. While task 1008 defaults to conditional availability (disabled in production via NODE_ENV check), this is a weak control. NODE_ENV can be misconfigured, and the endpoint name is predictable. Even with the conditional check, the route file ships in the production bundle. The response reveals internal component names and architecture patterns.
findings.recommendation: Implement defense-in-depth: (1) Use the NODE_ENV conditional as specified. (2) Additionally, require an API key or bearer token (from an environment variable) to access the endpoint. (3) Consider using Next.js middleware to block the route pattern in production rather than relying solely on in-handler checks. (4) Add the route to a .env-based allowlist so it can be explicitly enabled per environment. (5) Add a note in task 1008 that this route MUST be verified as inaccessible during production deployment smoke tests.

findings.title: No pod security standards or container security hardening specified
findings.description: The scale tasks (1012-1017) deploy production Kubernetes infrastructure but do not mention Pod Security Standards (PSS), Pod Security Admission, securityContext configurations (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop ALL capabilities), or container image scanning. NATS and application containers will run with default (potentially privileged) security contexts.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated infrastructure hardening task or subtasks within existing tasks: (1) Enforce Pod Security Standards at 'restricted' level for all namespaces. (2) Set securityContext on all pods: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, drop ALL capabilities. (3) Integrate container image scanning (Trivy, Snyk Container) into CI/CD pipeline. (4) Use distroless or minimal base images for all containers.

findings.title: No dependency pinning, auditing, or container image provenance verification
findings.description: Task 1001 scaffolds the project with `npx create-next-app@latest` which installs unpinned latest versions. No task mentions running `npm audit`, pinning exact dependency versions, using a lockfile strategy, or verifying container base image digests. The plan installs Playwright and axe-core without version pinning. Supply chain attacks through compromised npm packages or container images are a significant risk.
findings.recommendation: Add these requirements to task 1001: (1) Use `--save-exact` for all npm installs or configure `.npmrc` with `save-exact=true`. (2) Commit `package-lock.json` and use `npm ci` in CI. (3) Add `npm audit --audit-level=high` to CI pipeline. (4) Pin the Next.js version explicitly (e.g., 14.2.x, not @latest). For infrastructure tasks: (5) Reference container images by SHA256 digest, not just tag. (6) Use only images from trusted registries (official NATS, OpenTelemetry, Jaeger images). (7) Add a Dependabot or Renovate configuration for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: Ingress security headers and rate limiting insufficiently specified
findings.description: Task 1014 mentions 'set secure headers, rate limiting' but does not specify which headers or what rate limits. Critical security headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) need explicit configuration. Rate limiting parameters need to be defined to prevent abuse.
findings.recommendation: Expand task 1014 with specific requirements: (1) Configure these headers at ingress level: Strict-Transport-Security (max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Permissions-Policy (disable unused APIs). (2) Implement Content-Security-Policy with specific directives (script-src, style-src, img-src, connect-src) — this requires coordination with Next.js inline styles/scripts. (3) Define rate limiting: e.g., 100 requests/minute per IP for API routes, 1000/minute for static assets. (4) Add a test subtask to verify all headers are present using `curl -I`.

findings.title: No security event logging, audit trail, or anomaly detection
findings.description: Task 1016 deploys OpenTelemetry and Jaeger for tracing, and task 1017 sets up alerting, but neither addresses security-specific observability: no audit logging for API access, no security event logging (failed auth attempts, rate limit violations, network policy denials), no anomaly detection, and no log aggregation strategy. Jaeger is for distributed tracing, not security monitoring.
findings.recommendation: Add security observability requirements: (1) Deploy a log aggregation stack (e.g., Loki + Grafana, or EFK) alongside Jaeger. (2) Configure NATS to emit audit logs for connection events and authorization failures. (3) Configure ingress controller to log all 4xx/5xx responses with client IP, path, and user-agent. (4) Add alert rules in task 1017 for: unusual traffic spikes, repeated 403/401 responses, network policy deny events from Cilium. (5) Ensure logs are shipped to a tamper-evident store with retention policy for compliance.

findings.title: No input validation or sanitization framework for future API routes
findings.description: The current plan only has one API route (/api/snapshot) which takes no input. However, the infrastructure being deployed (NATS, ingress, Kubernetes) suggests this is a foundation for a larger application. There is no task establishing input validation patterns, schema validation libraries (Zod, Joi), or XSS/injection prevention middleware that future API routes would inherit.
findings.recommendation: While not critical for the current marketing-only scope, add a foundational task or documented decision for input validation: (1) Install and configure Zod for runtime schema validation. (2) Create a middleware pattern or utility function for validating request bodies/params. (3) Document in the task docs (1009) that all future API routes MUST validate inputs using the established pattern. (4) Configure Next.js security headers (CSP) at the application level as a second layer beyond ingress.

findings.title: NATS JetStream persistence lacks encryption at rest specification
findings.description: Task 1012 configures JetStream with persistent volume claims for message durability but does not specify encryption at rest for the stored messages. Depending on the data flowing through NATS, unencrypted PVCs could expose sensitive data if the underlying storage is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Add to task 1012: (1) Specify that PVCs should use a StorageClass with encryption at rest enabled (e.g., AWS EBS encryption, GCP PD encryption, or dm-crypt). (2) If the cloud provider's default StorageClass doesn't encrypt, create an encrypted StorageClass and reference it explicitly. (3) Document what data classification levels are acceptable for NATS messages without application-layer encryption.

findings.title: Cloudflare CDN configuration needs security-specific settings
findings.description: Task 1014 mentions integrating Cloudflare CDN for static asset caching and DDoS protection but doesn't specify security-relevant Cloudflare configurations: SSL mode (Full Strict), WAF rules, bot management, challenge pages for suspicious traffic, or cache-control headers to prevent caching of dynamic/authenticated responses.
findings.recommendation: Add Cloudflare-specific security subtasks: (1) Set SSL/TLS encryption mode to 'Full (Strict)'. (2) Enable Cloudflare WAF with OWASP Core Rule Set. (3) Configure bot management to challenge suspicious automated traffic. (4) Set cache-control headers to ensure API responses (e.g., /api/*) are never cached by CDN. (5) Enable DNSSEC on the domain. (6) Configure Cloudflare firewall rules to geo-block if applicable.

strengths: The /api/snapshot endpoint defaults to being disabled in production via NODE_ENV check, showing awareness of information exposure risks.
Network policies (task 1015) follow a deny-all-by-default approach with explicit allowlisting, which is the correct security posture.
TLS is explicitly required for NATS cluster and client connections (task 1013), with cert-manager for automated certificate lifecycle.

The plan uses semantic HTML and accessibility-first design principles (4.5:1 contrast ratios, focus indicators), which while primarily UX concerns, also reduce certain classes of accessibility-related legal risk.
The task structure separates infrastructure concerns (scale tasks) from application code, enabling independent security review and hardening of each layer.
TypeScript strict mode is enabled from project initialization (task 1001), providing type safety that prevents certain classes of runtime errors.
summary: The plan has a HIGH overall risk level primarily due to the complete absence of authentication/authorization for both the application and NATS messaging infrastructure, missing secrets management strategy, and lack of security observability. The frontend marketing site itself has limited attack surface, but the production infrastructure being deployed (NATS cluster, Kubernetes networking, ingress, CDN) lacks critical security controls including pod security standards, container image scanning, dependency auditing, and security event logging. The plan does well with network isolation (deny-all policies) and TLS enforcement, but these strengths are undermined by the authentication and secrets management gaps. Ten findings were identified, with two rated high severity — both related to authentication and secrets management — that should be addressed before production deployment.
